text: corner to take my seat. What have I done in the short span of time I’ve been here? Before I can sit down, the window slides back open. “You, danger boy, enough already, I can’t leave you alone for a second. Just come on back, already. Jesus,” she mutters, sliding it closed, a simple white door to the left popping open. # Jeremiah sits behind a large mahogany desk that looks as though it might have been on a pirate ship, in the captain’s quarters, or perhaps the library of a king. It is carved with intricate moldings and inlays, the legs ending with claws, the heads of various beasts carved into each corner. The walls on three sides of this room are painted a neutral shade of beige, but the shelves behind him are book shelves from the floor to the ceiling, filled to overflowing with ancient texts. Bound in leather, embossed with gold, stamped with metals and glittering stones, these are not the textbooks, novels, and paperbacks you’d find at the corner bookstores. These are tomes, manifestos, and grimoires— The Codex Gigas, The Book of Soyga, The Picatrix al Hikam . The words and titles mean nothing to me, but in the presence of these texts, I feel both uneasy, and at peace. “I’m glad you came,” he says. “I’m sure you have so many questions, but I’m afraid I probably won’t be able to answer them. My clients ask for, and deserve, their privacy. In fact, I apologize for the events outside. I should have known your presence would cause a ripple, as well, but that can’t be avoided, I suppose. You have to come to me when you are ready, and if I only let one patient in at a time, well, we’d be here for all of eternity. No rest for the wicked, right?” I nod my head. “Why am I here, Jeremiah? What is it you do here?” He leans back in his black leather desk chair, and steeples his fingers. “I am a purveyor of goods. I am a healer. I am a priest. I see in others that which they cannot. I see in others those toxins that continue to destroy. When I do my job right, when the moons and planets align, I can create a chemical reaction, an algorithm, a formula that takes one essence from over HERE and moves it over THERE, with very different results. You, my friend, are toxic. You have been anointed with a darkness that has seeped into your bones, quite literally, and it poisons everyone and everything around you.” Jeremiah pauses for a moment, and then gets up to light a series of lavender candles, one in each corner of the room. He then takes a bundle of sage off of a shelf, lights it, blunts it, and then sets it in a large ceramic ashtray in the shape of a skull, pushing it across the desk, away from himself, and toward me. He sits back down and relaxes. “Much better,” he says. “I should have done that before you came in. I have other precautions on hand, but I underestimated your presence, my friend.” He motions around the room to scrolls that hang on the wall, a grouping of crosses, and a woven rug with geometrical patterns. I notice for the first time the rosemary bushes with their purple flowers, green and yellow plants with their sword-like leaves pushing up towards the sky, and what looks like basil growing in a pot with other herbs. “I have a proposal,” he says. “I would like to extract your bone marrow. It is a painful process, not when we extract it, but when the anesthesia wears off. But that’s part of why I’m doing this. Your marrow, if I’m correct, and I usually am, can help others. It can tamp down memories, bad memories, it can help to numb individuals to the pain that they carry with them, and it can allow the body, mind, and soul to heal.” I listen to what he has to say. But I’m not sure if I care. “Why would I do this? What’s in it for me?” Jeremiah nods his head, frowning, as if I’ve let him down. He sighs, and continues. “There will be of course a financial compensation which I think you will find quite adequate. But more importantly, this compound that I manufacture, it can be used for your suffering as well. I can help you to move on. And I think that’s what you want to do. To be able to finally forgive yourself. To heal.” I nod my head. I think of the guy outside, and his discomfort at my reaction, the woman that I spoke to, how she slowly disappeared, and the large man in the corner, with the tattoos that weren’t for me. I decide to do what he says. And in that relief, I sob into my shaking hands. # In the shadows, there is giggling, the laughter of my son, a click, and the sound of a heavy metal door opening, and then tiny footsteps running away, fading down the hallway. Later there is a loud noise, a bang, and even later still, the closing of a door, a bath being run, and a heavy sigh from my distant wife, followed by gentle sobbing. # The procedure works. They extract my marrow, and use it as a catalyst for some compound, which can be turned into a treatment for those that suffer, like myself. I have forgotten that which I needed to forget, and in donating my rotten core to science, am able to heal others. It allows me to rise up out of the darkness and exist in the light again. I sell the bar to some local musicians and they start remodeling right away. I begin dating a nice woman who lives down the street, and I’m able to move on from the trauma of my past.
